node.text: ◆ signal: CUTLASS Tutorial: Efficient GEMM kernel designs with Pipelining (Hieu Pham/Colfax) — Software pipelining to overlap memory copy with compute is directly applicable to the per-block panel factorization and trailing-matrix GEMM updates that dominate batched Cholesky throughput on A100/H100.

◆ signal: Journey to Understanding FlashMLA: CUTLASS/CuTe Terminology Guide — A CuTe layout/atom/collective primer is the fastest on-ramp to writing a custom fused Cholesky kernel using CUTLASS building blocks rather than cuSOLVER, useful for the small-n high-batch regime where library launch overhead dominates.

receipts.quote: insists on mechanism — annotates architecture papers with 'how much scaffolding is provided beforehand?'

node.text: ◆ signal: Making Deep Learning Go Brrrr From First Principles (Horace He) — The compute/memory/overhead taxonomy tells you the small-batch small-n Cholesky entries are launch-overhead-bound (favor a single fused batched kernel) while the 32768x32768 entry is compute-bound (favor blocked GEMM-heavy right-looking algorithm).

◆ signal: Understanding GPU bottlenecks with a visualisation (Fleetwood) — Roofline-style visualization helps decide per-benchmark-shape which regime (occupancy vs bandwidth vs latency) each Cholesky entry lands in, guiding the geomean-optimal kernel selection strategy.

◆ signal: CUDA Programming Course (Elliot Arledge, freeCodeCamp) — Foundational CUDA course covering shared-memory tiling and warp-level primitives needed to hand-roll the n<=32 batched Cholesky kernel where one matrix fits in registers/shared memory per warp.

node.text: ◆ signal: A Fast Batched Cholesky Factorization on a GPU (Dongarra et al.) — The recursive-blocked design—left-looking recursive panel factorization fused into one kernel plus right-looking SYRK trailing update, with a custom batched dsyrk that writes only the lower triangle (4x faster than cuBLAS-GEMM-based syrk)—is the canonical recipe to beat cuSOLVER on this leaderboard.

receipts.quote: how much scaffolding is provided beforehand?

node.text: ◆ signal: Fast Cholesky factorization on GPUs for batch and native modes in MAGMA — A single unified device-routine code base serves both the small-batch (batch) and single-large-matrix (native) modes, with special sub-warp-sized 'tiny' matrix designs (n<=32) delivering up to 11.8x speedups on V100—exactly matching this leaderboard's dual regime (n=32 batch=4096 vs n=32768 batch=1).

node.text: ◆ signal: MAGMA Batched (one thread block per matrix; big-tile vs classic settings) — Assigning one thread block per matrix with intra-block barrier sync ('big-tile' setting) wins for panel factorization while the 'classic' multi-block GEMM setting wins for large trailing updates—so the geomean-optimal strategy dispatches different kernel configs per benchmark shape.

text: generating root ideas from the seed set…

receipts.quote: one thread block per matrix with intra-block barrier sync ('big-tile' setting) wins for panel factorization
special sub-warp-sized 'tiny' matrix designs (n<=32) delivering up to 11.8x speedups on V100
the small-batch small-n Cholesky entries are launch-overhead-bound (favor a single fused batched kernel)
cuSOLVERDx exposes POTRF as an inlineable __device__ function operating on shared-memory tiles

node.text: One-block-per-matrix fused shared-memory Cholesky for the tiny/small-n high-batch regime
node.detail: For all entries where n<=256 (batch=4096/n=32, 1024/64, 256/128, 64/256, and the high-batch 640/512), assign exactly one CUDA thread block per matrix and factorize entirely in shared memory using intra-block __syncthreads barriers. Load the lower triangle into shared memory once, run an unblocked right-looking Cholesky (compute diag sqrt, scale column, rank-1 trailing update) with the whole block cooperating, then write back. This eliminates all per-batch library launch overhead — the dominant cost in this regime per Horace He's overhead taxonomy. For n=32 use sub-warp / one-warp-per-matrix packing so multiple matrices share a block and stay register/shared resident, matching MAGMA's 'tiny' special-cased design (up to 11.8x on V100). Tune shared-mem tiling and warp assignment per shape. This regime is ~half the geomean entries, so winning it dominates the score.

receipts.quote: right-looking SYRK trailing update, with a custom batched dsyrk that writes only the lower triangle (4x faster than cuBLAS-GEMM-based syrk)
Software pipelining to overlap memory copy with compute is directly applicable to the ... trailing-matrix GEMM updates that dominate batched Cholesky throughput
the 32768x32768 entry is compute-bound (favor blocked GEMM-heavy right-looking algorithm)

node.text: Right-looking blocked GEMM-heavy factorization for the single large matrix (n>=4096) entries
node.detail: For n=4096, 8192, 16384, 32768 (batch 1-2), the problem is compute-bound, so lean on a classic right-looking blocked Cholesky: partition into NBxNB tiles, factor the diagonal panel (POTRF), TRSM the panel below, then SYRK/GEMM the trailing submatrix — the trailing update is the FLOP sink and should route to cuBLAS SGEMM (near-peak TF32/FP32) or a custom pipelined CUTLASS GEMM. Key win over cuSOLVER-as-is: a custom batched DSYRK that writes only the lower triangle (Dongarra reports ~4x over cuBLAS-GEMM-based syrk) and software pipelining (overlap tile loads with compute) per the Colfax GEMM tutorial. Sweep block size NB (128-512) against the roofline. Since these single-matrix entries carry equal geomean weight to a tiny-matrix entry, a well-tuned blocked path is essential but individually lower-leverage than the batched regime.

receipts.quote: the geomean-optimal strategy dispatches different kernel configs per benchmark shape
Roofline-style visualization helps decide per-benchmark-shape which regime ... each Cholesky entry lands in
A single unified device-routine code base serves both the small-batch (batch) and single-large-matrix (native) modes

node.text: Per-shape kernel dispatch driven by an offline roofline classification
node.detail: Rather than one algorithm, build a dispatcher that classifies each of the 15 benchmark shapes into a regime (launch-overhead-bound tiny-batch, occupancy-bound mid, bandwidth/compute-bound large) using a roofline model, and picks the pre-tuned kernel + config for each. Concretely: precompute a static lookup keyed on (batch, n) mapping to {fused-one-block, warp-packed-tiny, blocked-right-looking, cuSOLVER-fallback} plus tile sizes. This is exactly MAGMA's finding that 'classic' vs 'big-tile' settings win for different sub-tasks. Because ranking is geomean across all entries, no single entry can be neglected — a dispatcher that avoids the worst-case-per-shape is the geomean-optimal meta-strategy. Low-risk, high-leverage scaffolding that makes the other ideas composable.

receipts.quote: avoid per-batch library launch overhead—ideal for the thousands-of-tiny-matrices entries
researchers often cargo cult performance without a solid understanding on the underlying principles

node.text: cuSOLVER batched potrfBatched / cuBLAS baseline as correctness floor and fallback
node.detail: Ship torch.linalg.cholesky or cusolverDnSpotrfBatched as a first-cut baseline that is guaranteed correct against the property-based checker (lower-triangular, positive diagonal, low residual), then only replace shapes where a custom kernel measurably beats it. This is the pragmatic 'ship something that passes, then optimize the geomean' move — the checker validates reconstruction residual not elementwise match, so any numerically stable path qualifies. Value is as a safety net and as the measurement harness: every custom kernel is validated diff'd against this. Modest ceiling on its own (library launch overhead loses the tiny-batch entries) but de-risks the whole effort and matches the 'execution/ship' ethos.

receipts.quote: Inputs cover dense covariance-like matrices, planted spectra, diagonal matrices, damped low-rank matrices, scaled rows and columns, and tridiagonal SPD matrices

node.text: Exploit structured input classes (diagonal, tridiagonal, low-rank) with fast-path detection
node.detail: The input distribution explicitly includes diagonal matrices, tridiagonal SPD matrices, damped low-rank, and scaled rows/columns. For diagonal inputs, L = diag(sqrt(A_ii)) is O(n) not O(n^3). For tridiagonal SPD, a specialized O(n) scalar recurrence beats any dense O(n^3) factorization by orders of magnitude. A cheap structure-detection pass (check off-band mass) can route these to specialized kernels. Risk: the checker may not label structure and detection adds a pass; the benchmark may weight these lightly. But if any benchmark entry is drawn from the tridiagonal/diagonal class, the speedup is enormous and directly helps geomean. Worth a scouting pass to determine which shapes actually contain structured inputs before investing.

receipts.quote: left-looking recursive panel factorization fused into one kernel plus right-looking SYRK trailing update ... the canonical recipe to beat cuSOLVER
when panels fit in shared memory (small n) the extra reloads are cheap, guiding the crossover between fused-shared-memory and global-memory blocked algorithms

node.text: Left-looking recursive panel factorization fused into a single kernel (Dongarra recipe)
node.detail: For the mid regime (n=512..2048 at moderate batch: 16/512, 4/1024, 60/1024, 2/2048, 8/2048), use Dongarra's canonical batched recipe: a left-looking recursive panel factorization fused into one kernel, followed by a right-looking SYRK trailing update. Left-looking loads the k-th panel k times but when panels fit in shared memory those reloads are cheap (per the communication-volume roofline analysis), avoiding global-memory round-trips that a naive right-looking blocked path incurs at these sizes. This is the crossover regime where neither pure fused-one-block (matrices too big for shared mem) nor pure cuBLAS-GEMM-blocked (batch too small to saturate) is optimal — the recursive fused panel is the sweet spot. Higher implementation risk (recursion + fusion) but targets the awkward middle shapes the other ideas serve less well.

text: expansion 1/8: deep-diving "One-block-per-matrix fused shared-memory Cholesky for the tiny/small-n"

node.text: 1 Introduction
Cholesky Factorization on Batches ofMatriceswith Fixed and Variable Sizes
untitled
A Guide for Achieving High Performance with Very Small Matrices on GPU: A Case S
Batched one-sided factorizations of tiny matrices using GPUs: Challenges and cou
Fast Cholesky factorization on GPUs for batch and native modes in MAGMA

This is the highest-leverage idea in the tree for this leaderboard. The benchmark grid is dominated by high-batch small-n entries (n=32 through 256 plus 640/512), and the fresh MAGMA evidence directly validates the mecha
node.detail: (1) The parent lumps n=256 and 640/512 with n=32; the evidence is explicit that pure whole-matrix-in-shared only works cleanly to ~32x32 — n=128/256/512 need panel/blocking, so this is really two different kernels and the parent under-specifies the crossover. (2) Register vs shared choice is non-obvious and must be swept per size (Volta deprecates implicit warp-sync, so shared needs explicit __syncwarp/__syncthreads). (3) Correctness: unblocked right-looking POTF2 must guarantee positive diagonal and pass the reconstruction residual under FP32 for ill-conditioned (high cond) inputs — numerical care needed. (4) Occupancy: shared-mem footprint directly caps TBs/SM, so tiling is a real optimization problem not a formality.

receipts.quote: If threads read into registers, each thread has direct access to an entire row of the matrix. In order to access other elements, communication among threads is required, either by using shuffle operations... The second uses registers for storage and warp shuffle operations for communication.
target sizes are very small (less than 32 x 32), it is beneficial to keep the whole matrix in shared memory or in the register.
a different mindset and techniques are needed when matrices are tiny, and in particular, sub-vector/warp in size... speedups of up to 11.8x

node.text: Register-resident warp-shuffle Cholesky for n<=32 with N matrices packed per block
node.detail: For the batch=4096/n=32 entry (and reusable for n=64 sub-tiles), store each matrix in registers — one thread owns one row — and perform the rank-1 trailing update via __shfl_sync broadcasts of the pivot column instead of shared memory, per MAGMA's register-file version. Pack multiple 32x32 matrices per block (one warp per matrix) so 8+ matrices stay resident; registers are faster than shared (256KB register file vs 64KB shmem on P100) and avoid the __syncthreads cost that dominates tiny sizes. Sweep matrices-per-block for occupancy. This is the single largest-batch entry (4096) and geomean-critical.

receipts.quote: To address matrices larger than 32 x 32, the key idea is to divide the whole matrix into block columns... an n x n matrix will be divided into blocks of n x ib... allows us to load it panel by panel.

We prefetch data from A into register array rAk while a multiplication is being performed between register array rAkk and the array sB stored in shared memory... our kernel avoids reading it from the global memory and transposes it in place to the shared memory sB.

node.text: Fused left-looking panel kernel with register-prefetched update for n=128..512 high-batch
node.detail: For n=128/256 and the 640/512 high-batch entry, the whole matrix no longer fits cheaply in shared memory, so implement the MAGMA blocked left-looking design: divide into n x ib panels, load panel-by-panel, and fuse SYRK+GEMM update + POTF2 + TRSM into one kernel keeping the updated panel C in shared memory. Use double-buffering — prefetch A into register array rAk while multiplying rAkk against shmem sB, transposing B in-place to shmem to avoid re-reading from global. Autotune ib and recursion level m per shape. This closes the gap the register kernel cannot cover and is the correct mechanism for the larger small-n entries.

receipts.quote: If matrices are very small, we can assign multiple matrices to a TB instead of one matrix... Number of matrices per TB can be set dynamically during run time based on the matrix size... Up to 2.86x /1.34x speedups
one option is to use roughly 8+KB of shared memory per TB, with 7 TBs per SMX.

node.text: Tunable TB-level concurrency + early-termination scheduling across the batch dimension
node.detail: Layer MAGMA's runtime tuning knobs on top of both small-n kernels: dynamically set matrices-per-thread-block based on n (max concurrency for n=32, one-per-block for n=256), and since all matrices in an entry are same-size, skip vbatched machinery but adopt lazy scheduling intuition — as the right-looking factorization progresses the trailing submatrix shrinks, so reassign idle warps/threads (ETM-aggressive style: terminate idle threads within live blocks) to keep occupancy high late in the factorization. Budget shared memory to hit ~7 TBs/SMX target (8KB/TB) for the tiny sizes. This is the difference between a naive one-block-per-matrix kernel and a leaderboard-winning one.

text: expansion 2/8: deep-diving "Register-resident warp-shuffle Cholesky for n<=32 with N matrices pack"

node.text: warp/warp/examples/tile/example_tile_block_cholesky.py at ...
Autotuning batch Cholesky factorization in CUDA with interleaved layout of matri
Tiles — Warp 1.15.0
Autotuning batch Cholesky factorization in CUDA with interleaved layout of matri
A Guide for Achieving High Performance with Very Small Matrices on GPU: A Case S
Implementation and Tuning of Batched Cholesky

This is the sharpest known technique for the single most batch-heavy, geomean-critical entry (batch=4096/n=32), and the MAGMA 'tiny matrices <=32' literature is directly on point — register-resident, one-warp-per-matrix,
node.detail: Register pressure caps matrices-per-block and occupancy; clean one-thread-per-row mapping only holds at exactly n<=32 (n=64 breaks it); P100 register/shmem framing may be stale on modern GPUs; correctness of shfl-based triangular indexing is fiddly; absolute time saved on the tiniest matrices may be small relative to geomean weight.

receipts.quote: noncanonical data layouts, where consecutive memory locations store elements with the same row and column index in a set of consecutive matrices ... superior performance to traditional implementations for the case of very small matrices

node.text: Interleaved (transposed-batch) memory layout feeding the register-resident n=32 kernel
node.detail: Before the warp-shuffle kernel, restage the batch=4096/n=32 tensor into a non-canonical layout where consecutive memory addresses hold the same (row,col) element across consecutive matrices, so each warp's coalesced global loads/stores hit fully contiguous, coalesced addresses. Kurzak/Anzt (IPDPSW 2017) show this interleaved layout gives superior performance specifically for very small matrices. Measure whether the transpose cost (a cheap batched permute) is amortized by the coalescing win, or fuse the transpose into the load phase of the Cholesky kernel itself.

receipts.quote: For matrices of size 32 and smaller, there is only work for one warp. For larger matrices, there is work for more warps ... Increasing the number of warps per block increases the performance, up to four warps per block, at which point the maximum occupancy (100 percent) is reached

node.text: Two-warp-per-matrix register Cholesky for n=64 with cross-warp pivot exchange via shared
node.detail: Extend the register-resident model to the batch=1024/n=64 entry, where the clean one-thread-per-row mapping breaks. Assign 2 warps (64 threads) per matrix, one row per thread; the rank-1 trailing update needs the pivot column broadcast across both warps, so use __shfl_sync within each warp plus a single 64-element shared-memory staging slot for the cross-warp half — far cheaper than a full shared-memory factorization. Sweep matrices-per-block (Dongarra: up to 4 warps/block reaches 100% occupancy on Kepler; re-derive for target arch).

receipts.quote: we adapt common optimization techniques, such as optimal memory traffic, register blocking, and concurrency control ... a different mindset and techniques are needed when matrices are tiny, and in particular, sub-vector/warp in size

node.text: Occupancy-vs-register-pressure autotune sweep on modern (A100/H100) register file
node.detail: Rather than assume the P100 '256KB register vs 64KB shmem' framing, empirically sweep matrices-per-block (1,2,4,8,16 warps/block) and measure achieved occupancy under the actual per-thread register count of the n=32 kernel on the real target GPU. Each thread owns one 32-element row plus pivot scratch, so register count is bounded and predictable; find the packing that maximizes resident matrices without spilling. Follow MAGMA's autotune methodology (panel-width and thread-block-height power-of-two sweeps) restricted to the n<=32 regime.

text: expansion 3/8: deep-diving "Per-shape kernel dispatch driven by an offline roofline classification"

node.text: Performance Dispatch — Quadrants 1.1.1 documentation
Bridging Runtime Gaps in LLVM:
Seer: Predictive Runtime Kernel Selection for Irregular Problems
Are LLM-Generated GPU Kernels Production-Ready? A Trace-Driven Benchmark and Opt
RaMP: Runtime-Aware Megakernel Polymorphism for Mixture-of-Experts
Tessera: Unlocking Heterogeneous GPUs through Kernel-Granularity Disaggregation

The dispatcher is genuinely the geomean-optimal meta-strategy for this leaderboard: ranking is geomean across 15 heterogeneous shapes, so the winning submission must not blow any single entry, and a per-(batch,n) static
node.detail: Over-engineering: a runtime roofline classifier is unnecessary when all 15 shapes are known offline — a literal if/elif table suffices. Zero standalone speedup; value is parasitic on sibling kernels. Autotuning/measurement dispatch (perf_dispatch, MAB) adds warmup sync overhead that could hurt on the single-shot large-n entries if not cached correctly. Risk of premature scaffolding before kernels exist to route between.

receipts.quote: Different shapes converge to different optimal variants; context matters.
'classic' vs 'big-tile' settings win for different sub-tasks

node.text: Hardcoded 15-entry static dispatch table with per-entry compile-time-specialized kernel launches
node.detail: Skip the roofline model entirely. Since all 15 (batch,n) pairs are fixed and known, emit a compile-time switch mapping each exact shape to a template-specialized kernel instantiation (fixed n as template param enabling full unrolling/register allocation). Route: n<=32 high-batch -> warp-packed register kernel; n=64 -> two-warp; n=128..512 high-batch -> fused left-looking panel; n>=4096 single -> right-looking blocked; unknown shapes -> cuSOLVER fallback. The 'classification' is a literal lookup; the leverage is that fixed-n templating unlocks per-shape unrolling the generic path cannot. Measures once offline to pick the table entries.

receipts.quote: Exhaustive exploration (N × warmup samples) followed by permanent exploitation. Regret: constant, provably optimal for this problem class.
A four-parameter wave cost model selects the fastest configuration ... achieving 0.93% mean regret versus exhaustive search, fitted from just 10-24 minutes of one-time profiling

node.text: One-time offline MAB round-robin autotuner over kernel configs, frozen into the submission
node.detail: Use the LLVM poster's degenerate-bandit result: run round-robin exploration (N variants x 3 warmups) across candidate {tile size, TB concurrency, packing factor} configs for each of the 15 shapes during a local tuning pass, then bake the argmin-median winner as a constant into the submitted code — no runtime measurement, no warmup sync in the scored run. Convergence guaranteed in ~9 dispatches per shape; the submission ships the locked table so hot-path is pure cached launch with 3-6ns selection.

text: expansion 4/8: deep-diving "Fused left-looking panel kernel with register-prefetched update for n="

This is the mechanistically correct approach for the n=128..512 high-batch entries (256/128, 64/256, 640/512, plus arguably 512 entries), which the pure shared-memory one-block sibling cannot fully cover once the matrix
node.detail: Register pressure from prefetch arrays plus SMEM panel may crater occupancy at n=512, negating the fusion win; MAGMA's design is tuned for double precision on older GPUs and FP32 batched-small may not replicate the speedup; high implementation cost for a minority slice of geomean; numerical stability of fused POTF2 with FP32 roundoff on ill-conditioned (high cond) inputs; cuSOLVER may already dominate this exact regime.

receipts.quote: A fused (load, multiply, add, store) is ... HBM round-trip; the unfused version is three. Same total compute, three times the memory traffic.

node.text: SMEM-panel left-looking with cp.async double-buffered panel load, no register transpose
node.detail: Instead of MAGMA's register-prefetch + in-place SMEM transpose, use cp.async (Ampere+) to double-buffer the next n x ib panel from global into a second SMEM buffer while computing the SYRK+GEMM trailing update on the current panel. Store both the panel and its transpose explicitly in SMEM (cheap at ib<=32) to feed the GEMM as A and A^T without re-reading global. Target only the 640/512 and n=512 low-batch entries where the one-block kernel loses. This modernizes the 2018 MAGMA recipe to Ampere/Hopper async-copy, sidestepping the register-transpose complexity while keeping the HBM-round-trip elimination the fresh evidence validates.

node.text: ib and recursion-level autotune folded into the offline dispatch table rather than runtime
node.detail: Rather than autotuning ib and recursion depth m per shape at runtime, fold this panel kernel into the existing static 15-entry dispatch table (ev-7 sibling): compile-time-specialize the panel kernel with the empirically-best (ib, m) for each of the 3-4 shapes it owns (640/512, n=512, and the n=1024 low-batch entries where SYRK-heavy left-looking may beat right-looking). This eliminates runtime tuning branches, keeps kernels compile-time unrolled for register allocation, and only pays the engineering cost for shapes profiling shows this kernel actually wins. Bounds the blast radius of a high-cost approach to the entries where it earns its keep.

text: expansion 5/8: deep-diving "Right-looking blocked GEMM-heavy factorization for the single large ma"

Tuning rocSOLVER performance

Computing rank‐revealing factorizations of matrices stored out‐of‐core

This is a necessary but individually lower-leverage path, and the target's own framing admits it. The single-matrix entries (n=4096..32768, batch 1-2) carry equal geomean weight but represent only ~5 of 15 entries, and t
node.detail: Main risk: reinventing what cuSOLVER already does near-peak, spending hours for a few-percent gain on 2-3 entries. Secondary: TF32 trailing updates risk failing the FP32 reconstruction-residual checker for high-cond inputs — must verify accuracy tolerance before trusting TF32 SGEMM. Duplication risk with the cuSOLVER-fallback frontier sibling. The custom-SYRK win is misattributed from the batched-small regime.

receipts.quote: Beyond the size of 512, we assume a single matrix is large enough to saturate the GPU's computational throughput and users can call MAGMA's standard dgetrf
The blocked routine POTRF factorizes a leading block of rows/columns at each step ... and updates the trailing matrix with BLAS Level 3 operations (symmetric rank-k updates)

node.text: cuSOLVER POTRF with TF32-vs-FP32 accuracy gate for the largest single-matrix entries
node.detail: For n>=8192 (batch 1), call cuSOLVER's blocked potrf directly, but empirically determine per-entry whether TF32 trailing GEMM passes the reconstruction-residual checker across the input classes (planted spectra, damped low-rank, scaled rows). If TF32 passes at the tested cond range, enable cublasSetMathMode(TF32) for the ~2-4x throughput; if not, fall back to strict FP32. This is the highest-leverage large-n move because it exploits the checker's property-based (not elementwise) tolerance rather than out-engineering a vendor kernel that is already near roofline (ICL/MAGMA: single large matrix saturates the GPU, just call standard potrf).

receipts.quote: The multi-level blocked variant only applies the update to the next panel, but postpones the update of the rest of the trailing matrix after the k-levels of panels are factorized

the first iteration of right-looking algorithms usually requires the processing of the full matrix, whereas the last iteration requires just to process a very small amount of data (in some cases a b x b block)

node.text: Multi-level blocked trailing update to shrink the tail-latency of n=32768
node.detail: Adopt MAGMA's multi-level blocking specifically for the single 32768 matrix: at each step update only the next panel and delay the rest of the trailing update, accumulating k-levels so the eventual GEMM runs with larger k (higher arithmetic intensity, closer to peak). This targets the well-known right-looking inefficiency where the first iteration processes the full matrix while later iterations shrink to tiny blocks, wasting BLAS launch efficiency. Only worth it if the delayed-update scheduling beats cuSOLVER's internal tail handling; sweep block size NB=128..512 against the roofline as the parent proposed but with the delayed-update variant as the key differentiator.

The MAGMA tuning-knob framing is real and the receipts (icl-utk MAGMA batched papers) are legitimate for the tiny/small-n regime, but the specific mechanism this idea adds on top of the already-expanded one-block-per-mat
node.detail: Category error: cross-batch early-termination scheduling assumes runtime work heterogeneity that this fixed-shape workload does not have. Overlap: the useful content (per-n concurrency, SMEM budget, warp packing) duplicates the parent and occupancy-autotune siblings. The fresh evidence urls do not actually support the mechanism — they solve a different problem (SLA-bound heterogeneous inference).

Runtime Dispatch for Templated CUDA Kernels | rabbit hole 101
[2303.12374] Kernel Launcher: C++ Library for Optimal-Performance Portable CUDA
j8asic/SpeciaLUT

This idea is the mechanically-sharpened endpoint of the already-expanded 'per-shape dispatch driven by roofline' parent (ev 6) — it correctly drops the roofline pretense and admits the classification is a literal 15-entr
node.detail: (1) Zero standalone value — no kernel behind it means no speedup; it only pays off after the leaf kernels exist, so it's sequenced last. (2) Compilation blow-up: SpeciaLUT explicitly warns that compiling all specializations is slow; 15 fixed-n instantiations of large panel kernels could hit nvcc time/register-spill limits and the submission harness may cap build time. (3) Overfitting to the exact grid — if the leaderboard perturbs a shape the fallback path (cuSOLVER) governs and the templated advantage evaporates; but the objective states shapes are fixed, so this risk is low here. (4) Redundancy: this is 90% the same work item as the ev 6 roofline-dispatch parent already in the tree.

receipts.quote: Slow compilation of large functions. This compiles all possible specializations.
Compile-time parameters (via templates) allow the compiler to fully unroll loops and optimize aggressively.

node.text: Two-tier dispatch: constexpr n as template param feeding a single generic body, plus a batch-count runtime arg
node.detail: Rather than 15 fully-distinct kernel bodies, template ONLY n (and derived tile/block constants) so the compiler unrolls the inner factorization loops, but keep batch as a runtime grid dimension. This collapses the instantiation count to ~7 distinct n values (32,64,128,256,512,1024,2048,4096) instead of 15, cutting compile time and register-spill risk (SpeciaLUT warns compilation scales with specialization count) while retaining full unrolling. The dispatch table keys on n only; batch selects grid size at launch. Measure that unrolled fixed-n gives the promised speedup over a runtime-n loop on the n=32/64 register kernels specifically.

receipts.quote: Kernel Launcher compiles the kernel code at runtime by NVRTC... kernel selection and compilation only happens on the first launch of a kernel for a given problem size and on subsequent calls for the same problem size will reuse the compiled instance

node.text: NVRTC runtime-compile-on-first-call fallback for any off-grid shape, table for the 15 known
node.detail: Use the static compile-time table for the exact 15 benchmark shapes (fast path, unrolled), but wire a Kernel-Launcher-style NVRTC path that compiles a fixed-n specialization at first launch for any shape not in the table, caching the compiled module keyed on n. This makes the submission robust to grid perturbation without paying compile cost for shapes never seen, and avoids the cuSOLVER-fallback performance cliff for near-miss shapes. Only worth it if the harness permits runtime NVRTC and the first-call compile latency is amortized over the batch.

text: expansion 8/8: deep-diving "Interleaved (transposed-batch) memory layout feeding the register-resi"

node.text: Tensor Core MMA Swizzle Layout

A Fast Attention Kernel for MI300X, Written in HIP, Not Assembly - MoonMath.ai

Interleaved memory layout (Kurzak/Anzt IPDPSW 2017, and the ICL-UTK 2017 paper freshly surfaced) is a real, well-attested mechanism for the tiny-n high-batch regime — it turns thousands of independent small factorization
node.detail: Physical restage adds a full read+write pass over 16 MiB that may not amortize on a memory-bound tiny kernel; the 2017 evidence is CPU-SIMD, not GPU-warp, so transfer is uncertain; interleaving fights L2 cache locality (their own §3.5 caveat); risk of duplicating the warp-shuffle sibling rather than differentiating.

receipts.quote: the interleaved layout is a permutation of the strided memory layout
the transpose kernel writes V in exactly the order the PV matmul consumes, so each lane issues a single contiguous 16 byte load ... with no shuffle, broadcast, or permute afterward

node.text: Fuse interleaved gather into the warp-shuffle kernel's load phase (zero-restage)
node.detail: Do NOT materialize a transposed tensor. Instead, in the register-resident n=32 kernel, compute the interleaved global address at load time so lane t of warp w reads element (row_t, col) of matrix m at address base + (row*n+col)*batch + m — i.e. the strided/interleaved index — turning each warp's load of a given (row,col) across its resident matrices into a single coalesced 128B transaction. This gets the coalescing win of interleaving without the separate permute pass the parent worries about. A/B directly against canonical-layout loads in the same kernel; keep whichever wins on the 4096/n=32 entry.

receipts.quote: it may lead to a high cache miss rate ... To alleviate this problem, we divide the initial batch into small sub-batches (blocks), then apply the interleaved strategy within each block

node.text: Block-interleaved sub-batch layout to preserve L2 locality at n=64/128
node.detail: Apply the parent's interleaving only within small sub-batches sized so one thread block's working set of matrices stays L2/SMEM-resident, per the ICL-UTK §3.5 block-interleaved fix for the cache-miss hazard pure interleaving introduces. For n=64 (batch=1024) and n=128 (batch=256), pick sub-batch block = matrices-per-CUDA-block so the interleaved stride only spans co-resident matrices, keeping trailing-update reads cache-hot. Sweep block size as an occupancy knob folded into the offline dispatch table.

markdown:
# Exploration Brief — GPU MODE — Batched Dense Cholesky Factorization (leaderboard 776)

> Distilled from a recursive idea tree seeded by your curation. Each surviving idea is labeled against a no-corpus ablation — same model, same task, no saves, no scout, one fixed shot: **model-native** (the model had it anyway), **corpus-steered** (your corpus sharpened it), **corpus-dependent** (no baseline counterpart).

## 1. One-block-per-matrix fused shared-memory Cholesky for the tiny/small-n high-batch regime · 9/10 · model-native

*Ablation: Baseline idea 2 is the same one-block-per-matrix shared-memory Cholesky for small-n high-batch.*

For all entries where n<=256 (batch=4096/n=32, 1024/64, 256/128, 64/256, and the high-batch 640/512), assign exactly one CUDA thread block per matrix and factorize entirely in shared memory using intra-block __syncthreads barriers. Load the lower triangle into shared memory once, run an unblocked right-looking Cholesky (compute diag sqrt, scale column, rank-1 trailing update) with the whole block cooperating, then write back. This eliminates all per-batch library launch overhead — the dominant cost in this regime per Horace He's overhead taxonomy. For n=32 use sub-warp / one-warp-per-matrix packing so multiple matrices share a block and stay register/shared resident, matching MAGMA's 'tiny' special-cased design (up to 11.8x on V100). Tune shared-mem tiling and warp assignment per shape. This regime is ~half the geomean entries, so winning it dominates the score.

**Receipts:** [https://icl.utk.edu/files/publications/2016/icl-utk-909-2016.pdf] [https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1877750316305154] [doc_0800256bddaa9fe09044228b3ba496b3] [https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cusolverdx/get_started/potrf.html]

## 2. Register-resident warp-shuffle Cholesky for n<=32 with N matrices packed per block · 8/10 · model-native

*Ablation: Baseline idea 3 is the same warp-shuffle register kernel for n=32/64.*
*Lineage: One-block-per-matrix fused shared-memory Cholesky for the tiny/small-n high-batch regime → this*

For the batch=4096/n=32 entry (and reusable for n=64 sub-tiles), store each matrix in registers — one thread owns one row — and perform the rank-1 trailing update via __shfl_sync broadcasts of the pivot column instead of shared memory, per MAGMA's register-file version. Pack multiple 32x32 matrices per block (one warp per matrix) so 8+ matrices stay resident; registers are faster than shared (256KB register file vs 64KB shmem on P100) and avoid the __syncthreads cost that dominates tiny sizes. Sweep matrices-per-block for occupancy. This is the single largest-batch entry (4096) and geomean-critical.

**Receipts:** [https://par.nsf.gov/servlets/purl/10065617] [https://icl.utk.edu/files/publications/2018/icl-utk-1056-2018.pdf] [https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1877750316305154]

## 3. Fuse interleaved gather into the warp-shuffle kernel's load phase (zero-restage) · 7/10 · corpus-dependent

*Ablation: No baseline counterpart for fusing interleaved gather into load addressing (zero-restage transpose avoidance).*
*Lineage: One-block-per-matrix fused shared-memory Cholesky for the tiny/small-n high-batch regime → Register-resident warp-shuffle Cholesky for n<=32 with N matrices packed per block → Interleaved (transposed-batch) memory layout feeding the register-resident n=32 kernel → this*

Do NOT materialize a transposed tensor. Instead, in the register-resident n=32 kernel, compute the interleaved global address at load time so lane t of warp w reads element (row_t, col) of matrix m at address base + (row*n+col)*batch + m — i.e. the strided/interleaved index — turning each warp's load of a given (row,col) across its resident matrices into a single coalesced 128B transaction. This gets the coalescing win of interleaving without the separate permute pass the parent worries about. A/B directly against canonical-layout loads in the same kernel; keep whichever wins on the 4096/n=32 entry.

**Receipts:** [https://icl.utk.edu/files/publications/2017/icl-utk-1364-2017.pdf] [https://moonmath.ai/cdna3attention/]

## 4. Right-looking blocked GEMM-heavy factorization for the single large matrix (n>=4096) entries · 6/10 · model-native

*Ablation: Baseline idea 4/5 cover blocked right-looking factorization for large n.*

For n=4096, 8192, 16384, 32768 (batch 1-2), the problem is compute-bound, so lean on a classic right-looking blocked Cholesky: partition into NBxNB tiles, factor the diagonal panel (POTRF), TRSM the panel below, then SYRK/GEMM the trailing submatrix — the trailing update is the FLOP sink and should route to cuBLAS SGEMM (near-peak TF32/FP32) or a custom pipelined CUTLASS GEMM. Key win over cuSOLVER-as-is: a custom batched DSYRK that writes only the lower triangle (Dongarra reports ~4x over cuBLAS-GEMM-based syrk) and software pipelining (overlap tile loads with compute) per the Colfax GEMM tutorial. Sweep block size NB (128-512) against the roofline. Since these single-matrix entries carry equal geomean weight to a tiny-matrix entry, a well-tuned blocked path is essential but individually lower-leverage than the batched regime.

**Receipts:** [https://www.netlib.org/utk/people/JackDongarra/PAPERS/fast-batch-2014.pdf] [doc_5f11c747ee974d16234fa90c6b91cb9c] [doc_0800256bddaa9fe09044228b3ba496b3]

## 5. Per-shape kernel dispatch driven by an offline roofline classification · 6/10 · corpus-steered

*Ablation: Baseline idea 1 is regime-split dispatch, but corpus adds explicit offline roofline classification and static per-shape tuning.*

Rather than one algorithm, build a dispatcher that classifies each of the 15 benchmark shapes into a regime (launch-overhead-bound tiny-batch, occupancy-bound mid, bandwidth/compute-bound large) using a roofline model, and picks the pre-tuned kernel + config for each. Concretely: precompute a static lookup keyed on (batch, n) mapping to {fused-one-block, warp-packed-tiny, blocked-right-looking, cuSOLVER-fallback} plus tile sizes. This is exactly MAGMA's finding that 'classic' vs 'big-tile' settings win for different sub-tasks. Because ranking is geomean across all entries, no single entry can be neglected — a dispatcher that avoids the worst-case-per-shape is the geomean-optimal meta-strategy. Low-risk, high-leverage scaffolding that makes the other ideas composable.

**Receipts:** [https://icl.utk.edu/files/publications/2016/icl-utk-909-2016.pdf] [doc_ba165183b5f6f46b622bc9311ed2eee6] [https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1877750316305154]

## 6. cuSOLVER batched potrfBatched / cuBLAS baseline as correctness floor and fallback · 6/10 · corpus-steered

*Ablation: Baseline idea 5 uses cuSOLVER for large n; corpus frames it as correctness floor/fallback across all shapes, more explicitly a baseline strategy.*

Ship torch.linalg.cholesky or cusolverDnSpotrfBatched as a first-cut baseline that is guaranteed correct against the property-based checker (lower-triangular, positive diagonal, low residual), then only replace shapes where a custom kernel measurably beats it. This is the pragmatic 'ship something that passes, then optimize the geomean' move — the checker validates reconstruction residual not elementwise match, so any numerically stable path qualifies. Value is as a safety net and as the measurement harness: every custom kernel is validated diff'd against this. Modest ceiling on its own (library launch overhead loses the tiny-batch entries) but de-risks the whole effort and matches the 'execution/ship' ethos.

**Receipts:** [https://docs.nvidia.com/cuda/cusolverdx/get_started/potrf.html] [doc_0800256bddaa9fe09044228b3ba496b3]

## Appendix: the no-corpus baseline

Same model, same task spec, no corpus, one fixed shot. What it proposed:

1. **Regime-split dispatch: custom batched kernel for small n, cuSOLVER for large n** — Branch on (batch, n) at entry. For small matrices (n<=256) with large batch (4096..64), use a single custom CUDA kernel where one block factorizes one matrix, loading the whole matrix into shared memo
2. **One-block-per-matrix shared-memory Cholesky for the small-n high-batch tail** — Targets {4096,32},{1024,64},{256,128},{64,256}. Assign one thread block per matrix; cooperatively load the lower triangle into shared memory (n<=128 fits comfortably: 128*128*4=64KB, use n=32/64 with 
3. **Warp-per-matrix vectorized kernel specialized for n=32 and n=64** — For n=32 (batch 4096): one warp = one matrix, each lane owns one column. Store columns in registers, use __shfl to broadcast the pivot column during rank-1 updates, __shfl reductions for diagonal dot 
4. **Blocked right-looking Cholesky with cuBLAS batched GEMM/SYRK/TRSM for mid-to-large n** — For n in 512..4096, tile matrix into b x b blocks (b=32 or 64). Loop over diagonal blocks: factor diagonal block (small custom kernel or cusolverDnSpotrfBatched on the block), then batched TRSM (cubla
5. **Single large-matrix path using cusolverDnXpotrf with 64-bit API and workspace reuse** — For the {1,4096..32768} entries, use cusolverDnXpotrf (the newer 64-bit generic API) which internally does blocked panel factorization over cuBLAS and scales to 32768x32768 (4GB fp32). Pre-query and c
6. **Fused load-factor-store with on-the-fly upper-zeroing to cut memory passes** — Across all custom kernels, avoid separate torch.tril masking passes. Allocate output L, and within the factorization kernel write only the lower triangle and explicitly store 0 to strict-upper entries
7. **Precision-safe diagonal handling and cond robustness without extra cost** — Because inputs span wide dynamic range (cond control, scaled rows/cols, damped low-rank), guard the sqrt of the diagonal: clamp A[j,j]-sum to a tiny positive floor (e.g. eps*trace) only when nonpositi
